  "active_set": ["R1.1", "R1.2", "R1.3", "R1.4", "R1.6", "R1.7", "RX.1", "RX.2", "RX.3", "RX.4", "RX.5"],
  "rationale": {
    "R1.1": "The section asserts a specific contribution claim (hybrid index achieving recall parity at compressed storage costs) and a gap statement implied by the three-direction taxonomy; reconstructing whether the framing is coherent and the gap genuinely motivated is central to reviewing this passage.",
    "R1.2": "The logical chain from prior-work survey → identified gap → FastNav proposal → benchmark claim must be internally consistent; e.g., the claim that FastNav combines anisotropic compression with disk-resident traversal must not contradict or duplicate what ScaNN/DiskANN already do.",
    "R1.3": "The 1.3× speedup at same recall@10 is a concrete quantitative claim attributed to evaluations on SIFT1B, Deep1B, BIGANN; verifying that these numbers are grounded in actual reported results (not overstated from the paper's own tables) is a core review responsibility here.",
    "R1.4": "This is the highest-stakes dimension: the passage explicitly positions FastNav against ScaNN, DiskANN, and NSG. Whether the delta is real, whether the 'strongest prior baseline' is correctly identified, and whether anisotropic compression + disk traversal is genuinely novel versus an assembly of existing ideas are load-bearing questions for the review.",
    "R1.6": "The prose is dense and the contribution sentence ('hybrid index that achieves the recall of in-memory graph methods at storage costs of compressed methods') is potentially ambiguous; writing clarity is relevant but not load-bearing for a section review.",
    "R1.7": "Four specific citations are named (Indyk & Motwani 1998, Jegou et al. 2011, Guo et al. 2020, Subramanya et al. 2019, Fu et al. 2019); verifying year accuracy, author lists, and whether coverage of the field is representative (e.g., HNSW is absent) is load-bearing given how central prior-work framing is to the novelty claim."

rationale.R1.1: The section asserts a specific contribution claim (hybrid index achieving recall parity at compressed storage costs) and a gap statement implied by the three-direction taxonomy; reconstructing whether the framing is coherent and the gap genuinely motivated is central to reviewing this passage.
rationale.R1.2: The logical chain from prior-work survey → identified gap → FastNav proposal → benchmark claim must be internally consistent; e.g., the claim that FastNav combines anisotropic compression with disk-resident traversal must not contradict or duplicate what ScaNN/DiskANN already do.
rationale.R1.3: The 1.3× speedup at same recall@10 is a concrete quantitative claim attributed to evaluations on SIFT1B, Deep1B, BIGANN; verifying that these numbers are grounded in actual reported results (not overstated from the paper's own tables) is a core review responsibility here.
rationale.R1.4: This is the highest-stakes dimension: the passage explicitly positions FastNav against ScaNN, DiskANN, and NSG. Whether the delta is real, whether the 'strongest prior baseline' is correctly identified, and whether anisotropic compression + disk traversal is genuinely novel versus an assembly of existing ideas are load-bearing questions for the review.
rationale.R1.6: The prose is dense and the contribution sentence ('hybrid index that achieves the recall of in-memory graph methods at storage costs of compressed methods') is potentially ambiguous; writing clarity is relevant but not load-bearing for a section review.
rationale.R1.7: Four specific citations are named (Indyk & Motwani 1998, Jegou et al. 2011, Guo et al. 2020, Subramanya et al. 2019, Fu et al. 2019); verifying year accuracy, author lists, and whether coverage of the field is representative (e.g., HNSW is absent) is load-bearing given how central prior-work framing is to the novelty claim.
